threats.title: Off-by-One Boundary at Exactly 360 Days Allows TGT Deposit for Zero TITN Output
threats.hypothesis_description: At exactly block.timestamp - launchTime == 360 days, onTokenTransfer allows the deposit (uses strict > 360 days check), but quoteTitn returns 0 (uses < 360 days in both rate branches). The user's TGT is transferred into the contract but 0 claimable TITN is credited. Additionally, claimTitn reverts at >= 360 days, so the user cannot reclaim anything. The TGT is permanently locked with no TITN received.
threats.investigation_rationale: Inconsistent boundary checks between onTokenTransfer (> 360 days revert), claimTitn (>= 360 days revert), and quoteTitn (< 90 days, < 360 days branches, else 0) create a gap at exactly 360 days where deposit succeeds but output is zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer: (block.timestamp - launchTime > 360 days) revert; quoteTitn: if(timeSinceLaunch < 90 days)...else if(timeSinceLaunch < 360 days)...else titnAmount=0; claimTitn: (block.timestamp - launchTime >= 360 days) revert

preconditions: block.timestamp - launchTime == exactly 360 days
User deposits TGT via transferAndCall at this exact moment
lockedStatus != Locked

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: onTokenTransfer uses > 360 days; quoteTitn uses < 360 days; discrepancy allows deposit with 0 output
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No check for titnOut == 0 after quoteTitn; user TGT is accepted but zero TITN credited

analysis_questions: At exactly 360 days, which code path in quoteTitn is taken?
Is there any recovery mechanism for TGT deposited with 0 TITN output?
Can this be triggered across multiple blocks at the boundary?
validation_requirements: Set block.timestamp to exactly launchTime + 360 days
Call onTokenTransfer and verify it succeeds
Verify quoteTitn returns 0 and claimTitn reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User deposits TGT at block where timestamp equals launchTime + 360 days. onTokenTransfer passes all checks. quoteTitn falls into the else branch (timeSinceLaunch not < 360 days) and returns 0. User's claimableTitnPerUser gets 0 added. TGT is locked in contract with no TITN return and no way to recover.
prerequisites: Transaction lands in block where timestamp == launchTime + 360 days

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: 100% loss of deposited TGT for affected transaction
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user depositing at the exact 360-day boundary
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no mechanism to return TGT to depositor

threats.test_coverage_gap: No boundary testing at exactly 360 days timestamp

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by checking quoteTitn output before depositing

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Accounting Mismatch: totalTitnClaimed Includes Bonus but totalTitnClaimable Only Decremented by Base
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, totalTitnClaimed is incremented by titnOut (base + proportional bonus), but totalTitnClaimable is only decremented by claimableTitn (base amount only). The proportional bonus is never reflected in totalTitnClaimable. This creates an accounting divergence where totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable no longer accurately tracks the contract's obligation, and the invariant totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable <= TITN_ARB can be violated after bonus distributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The totalTitnClaimed accumulator grows faster than totalTitnClaimable shrinks, creating inconsistency. While this may not directly cause fund loss in normal operation (since individual user claims use their own mapping), any off-chain system or future integration relying on these accumulators for solvency checks will get incorrect data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalTitnClaimed += titnOut; ... totalTitnClaimable -= claimableTitn; — titnOut includes bonus but claimableTitn does not

preconditions: At least one user calls withdrawRemainingTitn after 360 days
unclaimedTitn > 0 (there is bonus TITN to distribute)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Asymmetric state update: totalTitnClaimed += full payout; totalTitnClaimable -= base only
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable will exceed TITN_ARB after bonus distributions; accounting breaks for monitoring
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No special handling of bonus amount in global accumulators

analysis_questions: Does any on-chain logic depend on totalTitnClaimed or totalTitnClaimable for correctness?
Does the accounting mismatch affect individual user payouts?
Could this cause the last user's withdrawal to fail due to insufficient balance?
validation_requirements: Simulate multiple users calling withdrawRemainingTitn
Track totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable against TITN_ARB
Verify whether last user can still withdraw successfully

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Multiple users call withdrawRemainingTitn. Each adds (base + bonus) to totalTitnClaimed but only subtracts base from totalTitnClaimable. After all users claim, totalTitnClaimed = remainingTitnAfter1Year (correct actual outflow), but totalTitnClaimable = 0 and the sum totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB, breaking the conservation invariant for any monitoring system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct fund loss, but broken accounting may mislead integrations or monitoring

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test tracking global accumulator consistency through withdrawRemainingTitn

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable by comparing totalTitnClaimed + totalTitnClaimable vs TITN_ARB

threats.title: Post-year withdrawal can be permanently bricked via division-by-zero when initialTotalClaimable snapshots to 0
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawRemainingTitn initializes initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable on the first post-year call, then divides by initialTotalClaimable. If totalTitnClaimable is 0 at that first call (e.g., all users claimed before 360 days, but some user still has claimableTitnPerUser>0 is impossible; or more realistically: accounting was reduced to 0 via edge-case rounding/0-quote deposits that did not increase claimable, or state corruption), division by zero reverts and no user can withdraw remaining TITN.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code performs `(claimableTitn * unclaimedTitn) / initialTotalClaimable` without guarding `initialTotalClaimable > 0`. Even if logically expected nonzero, Phase D should validate all reachable states, including those created by 0-quote deposits and rounding truncation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdrawRemainingTitn snapshots `initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable` then divides by `initialTotalClaimable` without require(initialTotalClaimable>0)

preconditions: launchTime is set and >= 360 days have elapsed
At first call to withdrawRemainingTitn, totalTitnClaimable == 0 (or is set to 0 due to earlier actions)
At least one user still attempts to call withdrawRemainingTitn (needs claimableTitnPerUser > 0, so reachability must be validated)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: division by zero on first-call snapshot variable
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A single bad snapshot makes post-year claiming impossible (no alternate path after 360 days)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No require(initialTotalClaimable > 0) and no fallback distribution mode

analysis_questions: Is it reachable for totalTitnClaimable==0 while some user has claimableTitnPerUser>0 (should violate sum consistency invariant)?
Can totalTitnClaimable become 0 due to 0-quote deposits or incorrect decrements elsewhere?
Does any external token callback or owner action allow desync of totals?
validation_requirements: State-space test/fuzz around 360-day boundary including 0-quote deposits
Invariant test: totalTitnClaimable equals sum of claimables across users after sequences including withdrawRemainingTitn
Attempt to construct a state where withdrawRemainingTitn is callable and initialTotalClaimable==0

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a reachable path exists to make totalTitnClaimable==0 at the first post-year call, any subsequent post-year withdrawals will revert, locking remaining TITN in the contract and blocking late claimants.
prerequisites: None beyond reaching the state (potentially via normal use/edge cases)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Remaining TITN locked in MergeTgt; late claimants permanently unable to withdraw
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users intending to claim after 360 days

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown for initialTotalClaimable==0 handling

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor totalTitnClaimable approaching 0 before 360 days ends

threats.title: Constructor mints initial TITN to msg.sender, not owner/delegate, causing supply/ownership misassignment if deployer != delegate
threats.hypothesis_description: Titn constructor sets Ownable owner to _delegate, but mints `initialMintAmount` to `msg.sender` (the deployer EOA or factory), not to _delegate. If deployments are ever executed by a different deployer than the intended owner/delegate, the initial supply is assigned to the wrong party, which can break the protocol’s distribution and custody assumptions (e.g., deployer holds 1B TITN on Base while owner controls restrictions).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code-level mismatch between ownership authority and initial token distribution. Scripts appear to use the same deployer for both, but any variation (multisig delegate, factory, or relayer deployments) breaks assumptions.
deployment_assumptions: Deployer account (msg.sender in constructor) is not equal to the intended long-term owner (_delegate)
No immediate post-deploy transfer of initialMintAmount from deployer to owner occurs
threats.config_evidence: Titn.ts passes deployer as _delegate, which likely equals msg.sender in typical hardhat-deploy usage; however no on-chain guarantee is enforced in Titn.sol that msg.sender == _delegate at deployment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Ownable(_delegate) but `_mint(msg.sender, initialMintAmount)`

preconditions: initialMintAmount > 0 on at least one chain (e.g., Base deployment mints 1B)
Deployment pipeline uses a deployer different from the desired owner (_delegate) or deploys via a factory/relayer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: mint recipient not aligned with configured owner
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Initial supply custody can be separated from admin controls unintentionally

analysis_questions: Are deployments ever executed by a factory or different EOA than the intended owner/multisig?
Is the initial supply expected to be controlled by owner, or by deployer operationally?
validation_requirements: Review deployment addresses on each chain: deployer vs owner vs initial supply holder
Check post-deploy token transfer events for initialMintAmount movement

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a relayer/factory deploys Titn with _delegate set to a multisig, the relayer receives the initial supply and can refuse to transfer it, undermining the intended migration funding.
prerequisites: Misconfigured deployment or operational separation of deployer/owner

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss/mis-custody of the initial minted TITN supply on the chain where initialMintAmount > 0
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on planned bridging/deposit funding
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (operational recovery if deployer cooperates); otherwise low

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown asserting mint recipient equals owner

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare owner() vs balanceOf(deployer) immediately after deployment

threats.title: Admin-only deposit amount enforcement likely mismatches comment and can cause operational DoS (strict equality to TITN_ARB)
threats.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt.deposit strictly requires amount == TITN_ARB, but the inline comment says it enforces 12_500_000 * 1e18. This inconsistency suggests the intended deposit amount may have changed and the code/comment diverged. If the intended funding amount differs at deployment/operations (e.g., partial top-up, multiple tranches), deposit will revert, potentially preventing recovery from underfunding or insolvency.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even though onlyOwner, this is a correctness defect: strict equality to a constant can brick operational workflows if any adjustment is required, and the comment indicates confusion.
deployment_assumptions: Operators need to deposit amounts other than exactly 173,700,000e18 (e.g., to top-up after insolvency or to fund in tranches)
The system relies on deposit for solvency maintenance
threats.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts deposits exactly 173.7m (TITN_DEPOSIT_AMOUNT == TITN_ARB), so current scripts align; however no mechanism exists for partial deposits if operations change.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Hard require `amount != TITN_ARB` revert; comment claims a different enforced amount (12_500_000e18) implying potential spec mismatch

preconditions: Owner needs to (re)fund MergeTgt with an amount different from TITN_ARB
Owner has TITN allowance/balance to deposit

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: overly rigid equality guard + conflicting comment
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Could prevent remedial deposits/top-ups, leading to permanent insolvency/DoS after an incident

Check if operational runbooks require re-deposits or partial deposits

threats.title: TITN Insolvency via Unlimited TGT Deposits – No Cap Enforcement
threats.hypothesis_description: onTokenTransfer accepts unlimited TGT deposits and allocates proportional TITN via quoteTitn without checking whether cumulative allocated TITN exceeds the available TITN_ARB (173.7M). If total TGT deposited exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M), totalTitnClaimable exceeds TITN_ARB, rendering the contract insolvent. TGT max supply is ~1B (TGT_TO_EXCHANGE is 57.9% of it), so depositing the full supply would over-commit TITN by ~72%. Early claimers can drain the contract via claimTitn, leaving late claimers unable to claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental solvency defect: the contract mints obligations (claimable TITN accounting entries) without verifying they can be backed by actual TITN balance. Any TGT holder can deposit, making this exploitable by any on-chain actor. The resulting bank-run dynamic economically rewards early detection and claiming.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer increments claimableTitnPerUser and totalTitnClaimable by quoteTitn(amount) without any check that totalTitnClaimable + totalTitnClaimed <= TITN_ARB or titn.balanceOf(this)

preconditions: Total TGT deposited via onTokenTransfer exceeds TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (579M TGT)
lockedStatus != Locked and launchTime > 0 and within 360 day window
TGT total supply is ~1B (confirmed by constant comment: '57.9% of MAX_TGT')

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: quoteTitn: (tgtAmount * TITN_ARB) / TGT_TO_EXCHANGE — proportional per-deposit with no cumulative cap
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any TGT holder deposits TGT; if aggregate deposits exceed 579M TGT, totalTitnClaimable > 173.7M TITN but only 173.7M TITN exists in contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early claimers who detect insolvency can race to call claimTitn() and drain available TITN. If 1B TGT deposited in first 90d, ~300M TITN allocated but only 173.7M available. First ~58% of claimers succeed, rest get nothing.

economic_questions: What is the actual circulating TGT supply on Arbitrum?
At what TGT deposit threshold does insolvency begin?
How quickly could a bank run drain the contract once insolvency is detected?
validation_requirements: Simulate cumulative deposits exceeding TGT_TO_EXCHANGE and verify totalTitnClaimable > TITN_ARB
Verify claimTitn reverts for later claimers due to insufficient TITN balance
Test with realistic deposit patterns based on TGT supply distribution

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Permanent DoS via Arithmetic Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn(), the calculation `unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` will underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8+) if the contract's TITN balance at snapshot time is less than totalTitnClaimable. This occurs when TGT deposits exceed TGT_TO_EXCHANGE (ECON-H001), causing totalTitnClaimable > actual TITN balance. The snapshot is attempted on every call when remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0 (which it stays at if balance < totalTitnClaimable), permanently DoS-ing all post-360-day claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a cascading consequence of ECON-H001 that permanently locks all remaining user funds after 360 days. Since claimTitn is blocked after 360 days (>= check), and withdrawRemainingTitn always reverts, users have NO claim path. This creates a dead-end state machine with no recovery.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line ~117: `uint256 unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable` — no check that remainingTitnAfter1Year >= initialTotalClaimable. If contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at snapshot, underflow reverts permanently.

preconditions: Over-allocation occurred (ECON-H001) OR owner withdrew TITN via withdraw()
360 days have elapsed since launchTime
At least one user has claimableTitnPerUser > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable with no underflow guard
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Over-deposit of TGT makes contract balance < totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not a profit attack but permanent fund lock: all unclaimed TITN becomes irrecoverable

economic_questions: Under what over-allocation thresholds does the underflow occur?
If some users claimed before 360d, does partial claiming prevent the underflow?
Is there any recovery path once the underflow condition is set?
validation_requirements: Simulate: deposit > 579M TGT, advance to 360d, call withdrawRemainingTitn and verify revert
Verify re-initialization loop: remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0, every call re-enters init and underflows
Confirm no admin escape hatch (no function to manually set snapshot values)

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent once 360-day mark is reached with insolvent state

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Condition is latent; manifests automatically at 360-day mark
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All unclaimed TITN permanently locked; users who delayed claiming lose everything

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users who did not claim via claimTitn before 360 days
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of claimable TITN plus proportional bonus
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: totalTitnClaimable at 360-day mark (could be majority of TITN_ARB)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Track totalTitnClaimable vs titn.balanceOf(contract) over time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm: if balanceOf < totalTitnClaimable, subtraction reverts; and remainingTitnAfter1Year stays 0 causing perpetual re-initialization

threats.title: Permanent Transfer Restriction Griefing via Cross-Chain Dust Bridging
threats.hypothesis_description: The _credit function in Titn.sol permanently sets isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true for any address receiving bridged tokens, with no mechanism to clear it. An attacker can bridge a dust amount (1 wei) of TITN to any victim's address on a non-Arbitrum chain. This permanently marks the victim as a bridged token holder. When isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true, the victim cannot transfer their TITN (including legitimately acquired non-bridged tokens) on that chain, except to the owner, transferAllowedContract, or lzEndpoint.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isBridgedTokenHolder flag is monotonically set (false→true only) with no admin override or clearing function. The cost to grief is trivial (bridge fee + 1 wei), but the impact is permanent transfer restriction for the victim. On Base, this could lock significant non-bridged TITN holdings.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _credit line 103: `isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true` — permanent, no clearing function exists. _validateTransfer line 82: `isBridgedTokenHolder[from]` blocks transfers when isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true.

preconditions: Attacker holds any amount of TITN on a source chain (even 1 wei)
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true on the destination chain
Victim has a known address on the destination chain (non-Arbitrum, e.g., Base)
LayerZero OFT peer is configured between source and destination chains

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: isBridgedTokenHolder[_to] = true in _credit; no function exists to set it to false
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls OFT.send() with victim's address as recipient, sending 1 wei of TITN
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not profit-extracting; economic griefing. Could be used as extortion leverage or to disrupt competitors/traders.

economic_questions: What is the cost per griefing attack (LayerZero bridge fee + gas)?
How many addresses could be griefed per transaction?
Can the owner mitigate by setting isBridgedTokensTransferLocked = false globally?
validation_requirements: Bridge 1 wei TITN to a target address on Base; verify isBridgedTokenHolder[target] = true
With lock enabled, verify target cannot transfer any TITN on Base
Confirm no function exists to reset isBridgedTokenHolder

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: While isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true on target chain
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Targeted DoS; could disrupt whale wallets, market makers, or DeFi integrations on Base

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any targeted TITN holder on non-Arbitrum chains
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete inability to transfer TITN on the affected chain until owner globally disables lock

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify _credit always sets flag regardless of amount; verify _validateTransfer blocks with flag set

threats.title: Zero-Output Deposits Accepted During Late Decay Phase
threats.hypothesis_description: onTokenTransfer checks `amount == 0` for the input TGT amount but never validates that the output titnOut is non-zero. During the late decay phase (approaching 360 days) and due to integer division truncation, quoteTitn can return 0 for non-trivial TGT amounts. The deposit succeeds, TGT is permanently locked in the contract, and the user receives 0 claimable TITN. This extends beyond the exact 360d boundary (ECON-H003) to any point where the decay formula produces a zero result due to rounding.
threats.investigation_rationale: The missing `titnOut > 0` check after calling quoteTitn in onTokenTransfer creates a silent value trap. While precision loss is documented as accepted, complete loss of input tokens for zero output goes beyond precision loss into value destruction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 80: `uint256 titnOut = quoteTitn(amount)` — no check for titnOut > 0 before crediting. Line 142-144: decay formula with integer division `(tgtAmount * TITN_ARB * remainingtime) / (TGT_TO_EXCHANGE * 270 days)` rounds to 0 when remainingtime is small enough relative to tgtAmount.

preconditions: Late in the decay phase (approaching 360 days)
TGT amount is small enough that integer division truncates titnOut to 0
lockedStatus != Locked and launchTime > 0

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Decay formula: tgtAmount * 173.7M*1e18 * remainingtime / (579M*1e18 * 270*86400) — when remainingtime → 0, output → 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User deposits TGT near end of decay period; integer division yields 0
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit; pure value destruction for uninformed depositors

economic_questions: At what (tgtAmount, remainingtime) combinations does quoteTitn return exactly 0?
What is the minimum TGT amount that always returns non-zero for any remainingtime > 0?
Could a UI/frontend display a non-zero quote while the on-chain result truncates to 0?
validation_requirements: Calculate breakpoints: for a given TGT amount, find the time threshold where output becomes 0
Verify onTokenTransfer succeeds with titnOut = 0 and confirm TGT is locked permanently

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Final days of the 270-day decay period
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Per-user; limited aggregate impact unless many users deposit late

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users depositing TGT in the final portion of the decay period
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of deposited TGT lost for 0 TITN

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compute: 1 TGT (1e18 wei) at 1 day remaining: 1e18 * 1.737e26 * 86400 / (5.79e26 * 2.3328e7) ≈ 1.11e12 (non-zero). At 1 second: 1e18 * 1.737e26 * 1 / (5.79e26 * 2.3328e7) ≈ 1.28e10 (non-zero). For 1 wei TGT at 1 day: 1.11e-6 → rounds to 0.
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: The zero-output window primarily affects dust TGT deposits, but the missing check is still a code defect

threats.title: lzEndpoint Transfer Exemption Creates Irrecoverable Token Loss Path
threats.hypothesis_description: _validateTransfer exempts transfers where `to == lzEndpoint`, intending to allow bridging. However, actual OFT bridging uses OFT.send() → _debit() → _burn(), which bypasses transfer/transferFrom entirely. The lzEndpoint exemption therefore only enables direct ERC20 transfers TO the LayerZero endpoint address, which does NOT bridge the tokens – they become permanently stuck at the endpoint address. On Arbitrum (where all non-exempt transfers are blocked), this is one of only two allowed destinations, making it a likely trap for confused users attempting to bridge.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code exemption serves no valid purpose (bridging doesn't use transfer) and creates a permanent token loss vector. The dead code is especially dangerous on Arbitrum where users have very limited transfer options and may attempt to send tokens to lzEndpoint thinking it initiates a bridge.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 86: `to != lzEndpoint` — exempts transfers to lzEndpoint. But OFT._debit() uses _burn() (line 21 of OFT base), not transfer(). So `to == lzEndpoint` in a transfer() call sends tokens to the endpoint contract address directly, not bridging them.

preconditions: User on Arbitrum attempts to bridge by calling transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) instead of OFT.send()
isBridgedTokensTransferLocked is true (default)
User is restricted (all users on Arbitrum due to chainId check)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lzEndpoint exemption allows transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) to succeed, but tokens go to endpoint address not cross-chain
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User confusion: only allowed destinations are transferAllowedContract and lzEndpoint; user may choose lzEndpoint expecting bridge
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit; permanent token loss for the user. Tokens stuck at endpoint address with no recovery.

economic_questions: Can tokens sent to the lzEndpoint address be recovered by anyone?
Does the lzEndpoint contract have any sweep/recovery functions?
How likely is user confusion between transfer(lzEndpoint) and OFT.send()?
validation_requirements: Call transfer(lzEndpoint, amount) on Arbitrum and verify tokens go to endpoint address
Verify no cross-chain message is generated by this transfer
Check if lzEndpoint contract has token recovery capabilities

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time while transfer restrictions are active on Arbitrum

threats.title: withdrawRemainingTitn Snapshot Race: First Caller Determines Distribution for All Users
threats.hypothesis_description: The first caller of withdrawRemainingTitn() after 360 days sets remainingTitnAfter1Year and initialTotalClaimable permanently. These snapshot values determine the proportional bonus for ALL subsequent users. If the contract's TITN balance is manipulated before the first call (e.g., by someone sending TITN directly to the contract or owner withdrawing), the snapshot captures a distorted state. While donation doesn't benefit the donor (analysis shows net-negative), the owner or a coordinated entity could withdraw TITN just before the snapshot to reduce everyone's proportional bonus.
threats.investigation_rationale: The one-shot snapshot mechanism creates a critical window where balance manipulation affects all future distributions. The snapshot includes actual TITN balance (balanceOf) which can be influenced externally, while totalTitnClaimable is deterministic at that point (no deposits/claims possible after 360d).

proof_of_signal.pattern: Lines 107-111: `if (remainingTitnAfter1Year == 0) { remainingTitnAfter1Year = currentRemainingTitn; initialTotalClaimable = totalTitnClaimable; }` — One-shot initialization based on current state, permanent for all users.

threats.title: Precision Loss in Proportional Distribution Traps TITN
threats.hypothesis_description: In withdrawRemainingTitn, the user's bonus is calculated as (claimableTitn * unclaimedTitn) / initialTotalClaimable. Because Solidity integer division truncates towards zero, the sum of all userProportionalShare amounts will be strictly less than unclaimedTitn unless it divides perfectly. This leaves a dust amount of TITN permanently locked in the contract after all users have claimed.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the impact per user is small, the cumulative effect of truncation across many users guarantees that the contract will never fully empty, leaving a portion of the TITN supply permanently inaccessible.

threats.title: Public ERC20 withdrawals include TGT: attacker can force-send TGT to MergeTgt to block recovery accounting or confuse operations
threats.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt has no function for users to withdraw or account for TGT deposited; TGT accumulates in the contract. While this is intended (migration sink), it creates an operational risk: anyone can transfer arbitrary extra TGT (or other tokens) into the contract directly (standard ERC20 transfer), inflating balances observed by tgtBalance() and complicating reconciliation of 'migrated' amounts. If any off-chain operations (merkle refunds, audits, supply reporting) rely on contract TGT balance as a proxy for legitimate merges, an attacker can cheaply distort those metrics.
threats.investigation_rationale: Real-world operations frequently use on-chain balances for accounting dashboards and post-mortems. If the protocol later performs any off-chain distribution, burn, or reporting based on balances, forced token transfers can poison those measurements. This is especially relevant because MergeTgt has no internal accounting for total TGT received via onTokenTransfer vs direct transfers.

threats.title: Transfer restriction bypass by using bridge burn/mint path: Arbitrum 'locked transfers' do not prevent cross-chain exit
threats.hypothesis_description: Titn restricts transfer/transferFrom via _validateTransfer, but OFT bridge-out uses internal _debit (burn) and does not call transfer/transferFrom. threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical operational control gap: transfer locks often aim to limit liquidity and movements. threats.title: Changing transferAllowedContract Away From MergeTgt Permanently Locks All TITN Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt is deployed as the transferAllowedContract on Arbitrum (per arbitrumSetup.ts). On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), ALL transfers are blocked except for exempted addresses. MergeTgt relies on being the transferAllowedContract to transfer TITN to users in claimTitn() and withdrawRemainingTitn(). If the owner calls setTransferAllowedContract() to change it to a different address (e.g., a staking contract or DEX), MergeTgt loses its exemption. All safeTransfer calls in MergeTgt would revert due to transfer restrictions, permanently locking user claims. Additionally, MergeTgt is marked as isBridgedTokenHolder (TITN was bridged to it via sendToArb.ts), making the restriction apply on any chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a tight coupling between MergeTgt's claim functionality and Titn's transfer restriction system. There is only ONE transferAllowedContract slot. Changing it is a plausible admin action (e.g., to allow a staking contract or DEX). The consequence — permanently bricking all MergeTgt claims — is non-obvious and severe. The only recovery is calling setBridgedTokenTransferLocked(false), which disables ALL transfer restrictions globally.
threats.config_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: await titn.setTransferAllowedContract(deploymentMergeTgt.address) confirms MergeTgt is set as transferAllowedContract. sendToArb.ts bridges TITN to MergeTgt address, marking it as isBridgedTokenHolder.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Single transferAllowedContract slot. MergeTgt relies on being this address to transfer TITN. On Arbitrum (chainId 42161), block.chainid == arbitrumChainId is always true, so ALL non-exempt transfers revert. If transferAllowedContract changes, MergeTgt's from-address is no longer exempt.

threats.title: Owner Can Drain All User-Claimable TITN via Unrestricted withdraw Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function allows the owner to transfer any amount of any ERC20 token from the MergeTgt contract, including TITN designated for user claims. There is no check against outstanding user balances (totalTitnClaimable), no restriction on withdrawing the TITN token, and no timelock. The contract's solvency invariant (balance >= totalTitnClaimable) is completely unprotected from owner actions. This is not a bug in logic but a missing safeguard — the withdraw function should restrict token withdrawal to amounts not committed to users.

threats.title: TwoWay LockedStatus Defined but Not Implemented — Missing Reverse Exchange Functionality
threats.hypothesis_description: The LockedStatus enum defines three states: Locked, OneWay, and TwoWay. threats.title: Owner Can Drain User-Claimable TITN via Unrestricted withdraw()
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw() function allows the owner to transfer any amount of any ERC20 token from the MergeTgt contract to the owner's address with no restrictions. threats.title: Owner Pre-Snapshot TITN Withdrawal Corrupts withdrawRemainingTitn Proportional Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdrawRemainingTitn function takes a one-time snapshot of the contract's TITN balance (remainingTitnAfter1Year) on the first post-360-day call. If the owner calls withdraw(titn, amount) before this snapshot, the snapshotted balance is reduced. The proportional bonus calculation uses this reduced snapshot: unclaimedTitn = remainingTitnAfter1Year - initialTotalClaimable. If the owner extracts only the surplus (TITN_ARB - totalTitnClaimable), unclaimedTitn becomes 0 and no user receives a bonus. If the owner extracts more, it causes the arithmetic underflow in OPS-H002.
threats.investigation_rationale: The snapshot mechanism trusts that the TITN balance at the 360-day mark accurately reflects the pool available for distribution. Owner withdrawal before the snapshot silently reduces all users' proportional bonus.

proof_of_signal.pattern: remainingTitnAfter1Year = titn.balanceOf(address(this)) on first call; no protection against owner having already reduced this balance via withdraw()

threats.config_evidence: Deployment scripts set owner to deployer; no subsequent ownership transfer to a controlled admin system is shown beyond scripts.

proof_of_signal.lines: entry_points: MergeTgt.sol renounceOwnership() and Titn.sol renounceOwnership() listed as publicly accessible inherited functions
proof_of_signal.pattern: Ownable inherited management surface includes renounceOwnership(); loss of owner breaks onlyOwner workflows

preconditions: Owner calls renounceOwnership() on MergeTgt and/or Titn
Protocol requires further admin actions over time (launch, unlock transfers, incident response)
No alternative privileged control exists

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical state (launchTime, lock toggle, allowed contract) is only adjustable by owner, and ownership can be set to address(0).
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time; most damaging pre-launch or during an incident.

operational_questions: Is there a formal runbook preventing renounceOwnership, and is it enforced in tooling?
Will ownership be transferred to a governance system after deployment?
What recovery plan exists if ownership is renounced or lost?
validation_requirements: Confirm current owner address on deployed contracts and whether it is a safe admin system
Review operational permissions and proposed ownership lifecycle

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Owner renounces; protocol becomes unmanageable (cannot change locks/allowed contract).

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent freeze of intended operations (launch/transfer policy updates).
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Launch, merge operations, and cross-chain transfer policy management

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Most impactful before setup completion or during a crisis

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Inability to call onlyOwner functions; stalled setup steps.

threats.title: Cross-version Solidity compilation mismatch (0.8.9 pragma vs 0.8.22 compiler) may create unexpected runtime behavior across builds
threats.hypothesis_description: MergeTgt is declared with pragma ^0.8.9 while the project compiles with 0.8.22. While generally safe within 0.8.x, subtle semantic changes (e.g., ABIEncoder/optimizer behavior, revert string/bytecode differences, SafeERC20 edge behaviors) can produce different bytecode than expected by auditors or integrators relying on older builds. Operationally, this increases the chance of misverification and incorrect assumptions about deployed bytecode, especially for critical arithmetic in withdrawRemainingTitn and time boundary behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: Not a direct exploit by an unprivileged attacker, but a practical operational risk: mismatched builds can lead to wrong verification, monitoring, or signing policies and can hide defects during review if different compiler settings are used.
deployment_assumptions: Different environments (CI, local, third-party verifiers) may compile with different solc patch versions or optimizer toggles
Operators/integrators may rely on source/ABI generated under a different solc version than what was deployed
threats.config_evidence: hardhat.config.ts pins solc 0.8.22, while MergeTgt.sol uses pragma ^0.8.9 and IMerge.sol uses ^0.8.9, IERC677Receiver uses ^0.8.20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: solidity.compilers version pinned to 0.8.22 while contracts specify multiple pragma ranges (^0.8.9, ^0.8.20, ^0.8.22)

preconditions: Deployed bytecode compiled under 0.8.22 differs from bytecode assumed by reviewers/integrators using another 0.8.x compiler
Operational processes depend on exact bytecode/ABI matching (verification, allowlists, static analysis baselines)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Mixed pragmas across core contracts increase compilation ambiguity for third parties.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Primarily during deployment/verification and post-deploy integration.

operational_questions: Were the deployed contracts verified with the exact solc version and optimizer settings?
Do any external tools/integrations compile from source rather than consuming verified ABI?
Are there reproducible build artifacts (commit-tagged) for the deployed bytecode?
validation_requirements: Reproduce deployed bytecode using pinned solc and optimizer settings
Ensure Etherscan/Arbiscan/BaseScan verification uses exact compiler settings for each contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If mismatched artifacts are used, monitoring/verification can fail or misclassify behavior.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium—incorrect verification/monitoring and delayed incident response.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium—depends on ability to rebuild and re-verify.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Verification, monitoring, and third-party integration correctness

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Deployment time and any incident requiring rapid bytecode triage

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Verification discrepancies across explorers; ABI mismatches.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Tooling interpreting reverts/events incorrectly due to ABI mismatch.
